methodology_text:
The ZAP Model
Overview
The Z-Prospect Model isn’t gone. It’s just improved.

You can think of the ZAP Model — which stands for Zachariason-
Adjusted Prospect Model — as a V2. It’s like Charmander turning into
Charmeleon. We’re not quite to Charizard yet, but we’re getting there.

A lot of the same inputs are in this upgraded model, but some xes were
made to make things simpler and, in the end, better.

The goal of the ZAP Model is the same as the Z-Prospect one.

       It’s trying to predict how well an offensive player
       will perform in fantasy football across his rst
       three seasons in the league.
The thought process behind this mission is that you’re going to have a
general idea of how good a player is after his rst three years as a pro.
Late-career breakouts happen, but not frequently.

You can measure three-year success in a lot of di erent ways. Should we
focus on a player’s raw fantasy total during the timeframe? His average
points per game across the three seasons?

There’s no singular “best” way, in my eyes, but I used the infamous B2S
method for the ZAP Model.

Come again?

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                          8

                                                    fi
                                            fi
                                       ff
                                  fi
 B2S means “best two seasons”. It looks at a player’s rst three years in
the NFL, takes his top-two seasons in points per game in point per
reception (PPR) leagues, and it averages those seasons out.

Imagine a world where a wide receiver scored 11 points per game as a
rookie, 12 points per game as a second-year player, and 15 points per
game as a third-year one. With B2S, we’d throw out the rookie-season
number, and we’d averaged the 12 and 15 rates together. His B2S, then,
would be 13.5.

You can certainly run into issues with season-long points per game
averages. If a player only plays two games, but he scores 24 points per
game in those contests, are we to assume that he’d maintain that pace
across a full 17-game season?

That doesn’t seem logical.

In order to get genuine and reasonable averages, there’s a minimum
threshold of eight games that needs to be met by a player for his season
to count. Otherwise, the player is taking a zero for that season.

Going back to our example, if that wideout had only played ve games
during his second year in the league and at least 12 in his other two,
then his B2S wouldn’t be 13.5. It’d be 13. Rather than taking his second
season as one of the data points to average, we’d toss that and look at
his rookie season where he played more than eight games.

This is cool and all (maybe “cool” isn’t the right descriptor), but the
question you — I — need to be asking is: How do we actually predict a
player’s B2S? How do we get to the point where any of this matters?

I’ve worked with a lot of college football data over the years. With that
experience, I’ve been able to put together formulas at running back,
wide receiver, and tight end that do e ectively tell us who the best
prospects are in each class for fantasy purposes.

And I know that because of testing.

With any model, you want to make sure it works. Anyone can throw some

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                           9

                                                  fi
                                           fi
                           ff
      numbers together and claim to have an answer. If those numbers aren’t
     meaningful — if they’re not solving anything — then what’s the point?

     This is where draft capital starts to play a role in this story.

     Draft capital is simply where a player was selected in the NFL Draft.
     Bijan Robinson had top-10 draft capital last year. Puka Nacua was a Day
     3 pick, so his draft capital was much lower.

             Hypothetically, if NFL teams were brilliant at
             scouting, then we wouldn’t need tools to help us
             during our rookie drafts.
     We could instead just look at an NFL Draft board and select players
     based on where they were picked. The rst running back would be the
     RB1, the second running back the RB2, and so on.

     When testing my models, draft capital is the rst thing I test against. It’s
     the rst thing I look at to know whether or not the models are
     worthwhile. If the model is no better at predicting B2S than draft capital
     itself, then, again…what’s the point?

     Each prospect model that you’ll read about is better at predicting B2S
     than draft capital itself.

     This is probably a good time to point out who is being tested, eh? Which
     players am I looking at here?

     The wide receiver and running back models look at all players who were
     invited to the NFL Combine or who were drafted since 2011. There’s
     typically a ton of overlap there — if a player gets drafted, he usually was
     a combine participant. But we get one or two guys each season who sort
     of get drafted out of nowhere.

     The tight end model is a bit di erent, as it looks only at drafted players at
     the position. More on that later.

     Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
     Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                               10

                                         fi
                                   fi
                        ff
fi
 Where were we again? Oh, right. Draft capital.

The models themselves are more helpful to look at for B2S purposes
than draft capital itself. But what really takes them to the next level is
when draft capital becomes an actual input into the models.

Look. My goal isn’t to predict where a running back or wide receiver will
get drafted. It’s not to tell you if a player should get scooped up in Round
2 or Round 5 of the NFL Draft. There are plenty of analysts who are great
at that.

That’s not my game.

        My game is to help you win at fantasy football.
        Period.
                                                                                    Drafting Before
The last time I checked, there’s no medal awarded for ignoring draft                       the Draft
capital when making a selection during your rookie draft. Some rookie
drafts do happen before the NFL Draft goes down, sure, but the vast            Most leagues have their rookie drafts after
                                                                                  the NFL Draft concludes, but consider
majority of league drafts occur when Roger Goodell’s bro hugs are in the
                                                                                 joining one where rookie drafts happen
rearview.                                                                         beforehand. You can obtain some huge
                                                                                  values if you’ve done enough studying.

Why would we not use that information?

People may not want to hear this, but draft capital is the single most
important factor in the ZAP Model. NFL teams are putting millions and
millions of dollars into their draft-day decisions. They have teams of
scouts and analytics people helping them optimize those choices. I’m
just a dude living in my mom’s basement.

Why would I ignore that input?

The ZAP Model works without draft capital, but it’s lightyears better with
it. And it’s what really makes it a powerful tool.

To be clear, though, we’re not trying to just beat the NFL during our
rookie drafts. Being more predictive than draft capital alone is great and

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                                                                   11
 all, but it’s more helpful to be better than our actual competition — our
leaguemates.

Fortunately, the ZAP Model is better at predicting B2S than rookie draft
average draft position (ADP) as well. In other words, if you were to
blindly follow the ZAP Model instead of some ADP data — which is
formulated by the fantasy football masses — then you’d come out on
top more often than not.

Cool? Cool.

You ready to learn about what goes into this thing now?

The Wide Receiver Model
Breakout Age is a commonly used metric for wide receivers in the
numbers-driven prospecting space. It’s based on the idea that a player
who produces early in his collegiate career is likely better than a player
who only produces late. If a 10-year-old can play Canon in D on the
piano the same way a 17-year-old can, chances are, when both piano
players hit 20, the 10-year-old will be better.

To calculate Breakout Age, most look at the age of a player when he hits
a certain threshold in Dominator Rating. To put that another way, they’ll
look at how old a player was when he matched or exceeded a particular
percentage of team receiving production.

Despite Breakout Age being part of my earlier models — it got signal —
I’ve always had issues with it. The main one being that it’s too binary. It’s
too black and white. A wideout could be this close to hitting the proper
threshold, but, if he didn’t, he’d be lumped in with the Joe Shmo who
caught three passes all season long.

The ZAP Model at wide receiver doesn’t rely on Breakout Age. Instead,
I’ve developed a di erent methodology called Breakout Score.

A player’s Breakout Score at wide receiver is determined by his age
when hitting multiple adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt
thresholds.

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                               12

       ff
 Yeah, I’m going to need to explain that.

Receiving yards per team pass attempt is exactly what you think it is. It
looks at a player’s receiving yards in a given season, and it divides that
by the number of pass attempts his team threw. It’s one of the cleanest,
most-predictive metrics you can nd at wide receiver.

The “adjusted” piece is telling you that the player’s receiving yards per
team pass attempt rate is being xed — adjusted — for both games
played (prorated) and strength of competition.

In order for a season to count towards a wide receiver’s Breakout Score,
he needed to have played at least six games. Because, remember, we’re
looking at yards per team pass attempt on a per-game basis here. That
at least gives us somewhat of a sample to work o of.

As it pertains to competition — that piece isn’t as complicated as you
might think. A player hitting high-end marks in receiving yards per team
pass attempt at Alabama is much more impressive than a player doing
the same thing at South Alabama. No shade to any Jaguars out there —
we just know Alabama is a powerhouse program. Understanding where a
wide receiver played collegiate ball and where he produced (a lot of
players transfer these days) is key, and the model adjusts for that.

       Breakout Score has a much stronger correlation to
       B2S than Breakout Age does. And it makes the ZAP
       Model a lot more effective.
Another production-based piece to the model is kind of an obvious-yet-
untapped one: fantasy points per game.

Generally speaking, when a player has a higher fantasy points per game
(PPR) average in college, he’ll have a higher one in the NFL, too. When
that’s adjusted for the school he went to and the number of pass
attempts his team had, you get a Fantasy Points Score.

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                            13

                                      ff
                          fi
                     fi
      Lastly, when it comes to the production-focused stu , there’s rushing
     yards. This doesn’t get as much weight in the model as receiving yards
     per team pass attempt (Breakout Score) or fantasy points per game
     (Fantasy Points Score), but it does help a wide receiver out.

     When a player is used in a lot of di erent ways at the collegiate level, it’s
     likely because his coaches want to put the ball in his hands. In college
     football, you’ll often see some of the best players return punts and kicks.
     Or you’ll see top wide receivers get more work than usual as runners.
     That’s because those are low-risk ways to give playmakers the rock.

             This is probably why something as simple as
             rushing yards helps the model. Because it’s not
             only telling us that a player is versatile. It’s telling
             us that he’s talented.
     So, on the production end of things, that’s really what we’re working with
     at wide receiver. There’s Breakout Score, which focuses solely on
     adjusted and prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt. There’s
     fantasy points per game, which helps create a Fantasy Points Score. And
     then there’s just a little bit of rushing production sprinkled on top.

     A couple of other factors formulate a wide receiver’s ZAP score.

     Early-declare status is one.

     There’s been a lot of controversy around the predictability of early-
     declare status. Maybe there’s not a lot of chatter about the topic at the
     dinner table, but it’s out there.

     The big-brained thinking around early-declare status isn’t much
     di erent than something like Breakout Age: If a player decides to leave
     college early — if he declares early for the draft — chances are, that
     player is better than one who chooses to stick around another year.

     Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
     Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                               14

                                               ff
                            ff
ff
 Why would a wide receiver want to forgo his Senior season for the NFL?
Probably because he’s projected to get drafted fairly high, right? And the
only way he’d be slotted to go early in the NFL Draft is if he was good at
the game.

That’s why early-declare status gets a little bit of weight.

Name, image, and likeness (NIL) deals are more than likely going to shift
the importance of early-declare status as the years go on. When a player               Early-Declare
can make millions in college, he may actually want to just hang out there                   Changes
for a minute rather than going pro. So there’s a chance early-declare
status becomes more and more obsolete with future model xes.                 Over the last two seasons — aligning closely
                                                                                  to when college athletes started getting
                                                                              endorsement deals — we have indeed seen
For now, it’s an input. It’s not a massive input — not the way many in the           the percentage of early-declare wide
analytics world view it — but it’s an input.                                           receivers at the NFL Combine drop.

One last thing: Teammate Score. When a wide receiver plays alongside
high-caliber wide receiver teammates in college, it seems to matter. It’s
not a complex calculation, but know that teammate quality isn’t
irrelevant within the ZAP Model at wide receiver.

After joining all of these things — age-adjusted production metrics,
early-declare status, and Teammate Score — draft capital gets layered
on top. Those factors, then, create a wide receiver’s ZAP score.

Wait a second. Is that…is that athleticism’s music?

Some may be wondering when NFL Combine measurables come into
play.

At wide receiver, they don’t.

That may sound crazy. Shouldn’t a bigger-bodied, physically-gifted wide
receiver be highly sought after?

Not necessarily.

Subjectively, you probably would want an enormous and athletic wide
receiver on your team over a tiny and slow one.

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                                                                   15

                                                   fi
        But it’s very rare for completely unathletic wide
       receivers to get drafted early in the rst place.
Part of the reason size and athleticism aren’t a big deal in the wide
receiver model is because those things are likely already captured in
draft capital. And draft capital is in the wide receiver model.

Not only that, but wide receivers do di erent jobs on a football eld.
Cooper Kupp doesn’t win the same way DK Metcalf does. Keenan Allen
doesn’t do the same things Mike Evans does. Metcalf and Evans are
more physically dominant, and even though they’re highly productive as
pros, Kupp and Allen are, too.

If a player completely bombs the NFL Combine — and I’m talking like,
15th percentile marks across the board — you’d probably want to
remove that player from your consideration set. Chances are, though,
that same player won’t have good draft capital, and he likely wouldn’t
have had the best college production pro le. So, in the end, the lack of
athleticism and size doesn’t matter much because more important
dynamics weren’t going his way.

This is why it was di cult to knock Devonta Smith’s size when he was
entering the league in 2021. Was his low BMI a concern? Slightly, I
suppose. But Smith had an absurd production pro le while being
selected 10th overall. To me, the biggest knock was the fact that he
wasn’t an early-declare wide receiver, not the fact that he’s a skinny
dude. He ended up grading out as an elite player in the model as a
result.

The Running Back Model
De’Von Achane and Jahmyr Gibbs broke everyone’s models in 2023. Or,           Achane’s Unique
that’s what analytics haters on social media want you to think.                          Size
Size matters at running back. The vast majority of high-end running back     De’Von Achane is the only running back in
                                                                           the ZAP Model database who was drafted in
seasons in fantasy football have come from backs who weighed in            the top-100 of the NFL Draft while weighing
around 210 pounds or more.                                                          under 190 pounds at the combine.

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                                                                16

                                                    fi
                                         fi
                                    fi
                               fi
                          ff
        ffi
      And, yeah, a statistic like that is de nitely skewed by sample size. There
     are more running backs in the NFL who are above, say, 200 pounds than
     below that mark. And of the sub-200-pounders, most weren’t drafted
     very high. Most weren’t looked at as good prospects.

     But even when you adjust for all of that, size still comes in as a factor for
     fantasy backs. And it’s a part of the ZAP Model.

     This isn’t my opinion, either. I actually think plenty of 190-pound running
     backs could’ve been ne as fantasy assets through the years. It’s just
     that, in general, they don’t see as much work. Volume drives fantasy
     football, so if coaches have some inherent weight biases, then we need
     to as well.

             This doesn’t mean that smaller running backs
             won’t look favorable in the ZAP Model.
     Jahmyr Gibbs, in fact, had an amazing score in last year’s guide, and it
     looks almost identical within this revamped formula. Achane sat in a
     great tier, too.

     Because there’s more to this thing than just size.

     Just like at wide receiver, the running back ZAP Model has a Breakout
     Score. Instead of it being focused on receiving yards per team pass
     attempt, though, it looks at total yards per team play. It’s a similar
     metric, but total yards per team play considers all yards created by a
     player on o ense before dividing that number by the total pass and rush
     attempts made by a team in a given year.

     A running back’s Breakout Score essentially gives us an age-adjusted
     look at total yards per team play. It doesn’t get as much weight in the
     model as Breakout Score does for wide receivers, but it’s a new and
     important feature to a running back’s overall prospect tally.

     The only other production metric used in the running back model is an
     oldie but a goodie: reception share.

     Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
     Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                               17

                             fi
               fi
ff
      If you’ve noticed, all of the metrics I’ve talked about thus far are team-
     focused. They’re market share-related statistics. Something like yards
     per game might work in some models, but, typically, a metric like that
     isn’t all that predictive in college football. Because collegiate o enses
     di er dramatically from one unit to the next.

     The 2023 Liberty Flames, for example, threw the ball 21.6 times per
     game. The Hawaii Rainbow Warriors had nearly double that amount.
     Would it really be fair to compare a pass-catcher from one of those
     teams to the other? Of course not. Players on Hawaii would have an
     easier time gobbling up receiving numbers.

     With reception share, you’re analyzing the percentage of receptions a
     player had within his o ense. It gives us much more of an apples to
     apples comparison from one player to the next.

     In the ZAP Model’s case, a player’s best-season in reception share is
     what’s being counted. And that’s on a per-game basis with a minimum of
     six games played in a season.

     Unlike the wide receiver model, there is one NFL Combine measurable
     that the running back ZAP Model likes: 40-yard dash time.

     Don’t you roll your eyes at me!

     Technically, raw 40 times aren’t all that helpful. What is helpful is Speed
     Score, or weight-adjusted 40 time. Back in the day, Bill Barnwell thought
     of the metric after recognizing that not all 40-yard dashers are alike. It’s
     more impressive when a 240-pound running back runs a 4.4 40 versus a
     190-pound one.

     And the model mostly agrees.

     For reference, a solid Speed Score is typically around 100. When a
     running back is at or below 92, his ZAP score starts to get dinged. When
     a back gets over the 112 mark, the opposite happens. So the model
     really only looks at the extremes with Speed Score — most players are
     not impacted by it whatsoever.

     Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
     Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                              18

                                                          ff
                ff
ff
           Before adding draft capital on top, there are two more things that the
          running back model looks at. The rst is draft-day age. We have a
          Breakout Score for running backs, yes, but if a back is older when he’s
          drafted, that’s not a good sign.

          We’ve also got a Teammate Score. Similar to the wide receiver model, if
          a running back played with a reasonably talented teammate, his score
          gets boosted a tad — players in those situations may naturally have a
          tougher time posting strong numbers.

          So, to recap, it’s size (weight), age-adjusted total yards per team play
          (Breakout Score), prorated reception share, Speed Score, draft-day age,
          Teammate Score, and draft capital.

          Those are the ingredients that create a running back’s ZAP score.

          The Tight End Model
          My prospecting through numbers e orts, for years, had been channeled
          towards wide receivers and running backs. Those positions mean the
          most to fantasy football. Why wouldn’t I put a lot of time and energy into
           guring them out?

          The tight end position was feeling a little lonely.

          So, last o season, I set out to solve that equation.

          Tight end is just a di erent beast when compared to wide receiver and
          running back. Fewer of them are starting each week in fantasy football,
          so we’re working o of di erent sample sizes. Not only that, but the
          position has really transformed through the years — that can make
          samples more di cult to work with.

          During the winter and spring months of 2023 — we’re talking less than a            The Best in the
          year ago, depending on when you’re reading this thing — I concocted                     Database
          some form of a tight end model. And I shared it with Late-Round Fantasy
                                                                                           Any guesses as to which tight end was
          Football newsletter subscribers.                                                 viewed as the top prospect since 2015
                                                                                       according to the ZAP Model? Yeah. It’s Kyle
                                                                                                                            Pitts.
          It, honestly, was a hit.

          Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
          Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                                                                  19

     ff
fi             ffi   ff   ff   ff    fi   ff
           The model loved Sam LaPorta last year. It had Michael Mayer in a clear
          tier below him, when the market suggested otherwise. Luke Musgrave
          and Tucker Kraft were looked at favorably, too.

          There’s some back-patting going on here, yes. You’re not mistaken. And
          I feel weird about it. Anyone else feel weird?

          The reason for it, though, is because, without the 2023 season, I’m not
          sure I’d have the con dence to publish the tight end model here in
          2024. One year probably shouldn’t make or break that con dence, but
          here we are. I’m only human.

          As I mentioned, there’s a di erent type of sample to work o of with tight
          ends. Plenty of them end up just being o ensive line extensions in the
          NFL. There aren’t many true di erence-making pass-catchers at the
          position. And, in fantasy football, we’re starting just one of them in our
          standard lineup. That means fewer of them are relevant for fantasy
          purposes.

          All of these things — and data limitations — led me to working with a
          di erent overall sample compared to running back and wide receiver.
          Instead of analyzing all tight ends who’ve been to the NFL Combine
          since 2011, the tight end model assesses tight ends who’ve been
          drafted since 2015.

          The tight end model is a little more simplistic than the other two.
          Because tight ends typically aren’t giving us monster rookie-season
          numbers, measuring o of B2S isn’t as useful. The model, instead,
          measures against a tight end’s top season in PPR points per game
          across his rst three in the league.

          Draft capital, of course, is part of the tight end process, but it actually
          gets a little less weight than it does with wide receiver and running back.

          Athletic measurables get more weight.

          Well, one athletic measurable does: Speed Score.

          Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
          Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                             20

                                                               ff
                                                          fi
                                       ff
                                  ff
                             ff
                        ff
                   fi
     fi
ff
        Bigger, faster tight ends tend to be better at
       fantasy football than smaller, slower tight ends.
There’s no Breakout Score in the tight end model. Draft-day age is a
factor, but age-adjusted production is calculated a little di erently.
Rather than using a metric like receiving yards per team pass attempt or
total yards per team play to come up with a Breakout Score, the model
looks at yards per route run numbers. It takes a tight end’s average yards
per route run (minimum 50 routes run) across all of his collegiate
seasons.

That means the tight end model really just consists of draft capital,
Speed Score, yards per route run, and draft-day age. There’s more to it
— there are particular combinations and thresholds a tight end can hit
to boost his score. Like, for instance, a tight end being drafted in the
top-150 with a favorable Speed Score…he gets a bump in his prospect
score for that. Those are the types of things the model is analyzing. But
those four inputs make up the model in totality.

Historical Results
One beautiful part about prospecting through data is that you can test
and see if your process is working.

The table above provides the results of the wide receiver model from
2011 through 2021. The reason 2022 and 2023 aren’t part of the

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                            21

                                               ff
           dataset is because those players haven’t played three years in the
          league yet.

          The left column shows ZAP scores. As you can probably guess, ZAP
          scores range from 0 all the way to 100, with 100 being the most
          impressive. To make things consumable, the scores are bucketed in
          increments of ve.

          The middle of the table tells you the percentage of players from that
          score’s group to have averaged a particular points per game mark in
          B2S.

          Pretend a wide receiver scored 10, 12, and 17 PPR points per game
          across his rst three seasons in the league. And assume he played every
          possible game.

          As we know, with B2S, we’d throw out that average of 10 and focus
          solely on his outputs of 12 and 17. Averaging those out, the player would
          have a B2S of 14.5, placing him in the “14+” column (and the ones
          below “14+”) but not the “16+” one.

          Now that you’re a table-reading master, you can see that not everything
          is butter ies and rainbows with the wide receiver model. We shouldn’t
          expect it to be. It’s not going to be perfect.

          What’s most important is the high-level trends.

          As scores get worse, so do the B2S results. The vast majority of elite
          players — wideouts with B2S rates above 16 PPR points per game —
          have ZAP scores in the 90s. Meanwhile, it’s rare for a wide receiver
          under the 60 mark to give you good production.

          Something that needs to be said: If a lower-ranked bucket appears
          better than a higher-ranked one, it doesn’t mean you would want a wide
          receiver from the group with the lower scores. There’s some variance
          when arbitrarily grouping players.

          The running back visual looks a lot like the wide receiver one…

          Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
          Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                           22

            fi
     fi
fl
 When we get a running back over a score of 95, good things happen.
Only one running back with a score below that tally has hit the elusive
20-plus points per game mark in B2S: Alvin Kamara.

Lastly, we’ve got tight end.

Not bad!

Remember, we’re looking to predict a tight end’s top season in PPR
points per game over his rst three in the league. Since 2015, no tight
end under a score of 90 has been able to score 12 or more PPR points
per game in a season. That’ll de nitely change as we accumulate more
tight end data, but it gives us a really nice baseline to work with.

These tables are important to keep in mind as you read through the
guide. I’ll be providing scores for every relevant prospect this season,
and you can use those scores to get a rough idea of a player’s hit rate.

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                          23

                     fi
              fi
 Draft Capital Delta
I’ve talked a lot about draft capital and its in uence in the models. Since
it’s a big input, there’s a connection between how one of the models
sees a player and where that player was drafted.

Not only that, but good players are often beloved in both lm evaluation
and numbers-based analysis. Ja’Marr Chase has one of the best ZAP
scores in the database, but traditional scouting saw Chase as an
insanely good prospect, too. There’s an obvious overlap in the way the
model may view a player and the way the NFL — draft capital — does.

What about when there’s not?

That’s where Draft Capital Delta comes into play.

Draft Capital Delta measures the di erence between a player’s ZAP
score and where he was selected in the NFL Draft. It’s essentially
showing us how the models view a player when draft capital isn’t a
factor.

Take, for instance, Aaron Jones, owner of the highest Draft Capital Delta
in the running back database. Jones had a ZAP score of 79.5 back in
2017. Well, the ZAP Model wasn’t created then, but his score would
have been 79.5.

His draft capital score, based on going 182nd overall in the NFL Draft,
was 54.5. The di erence — the delta — between those numbers is +25.

That’s Aaron Jones’ Draft Capital Delta.
                                                                                         Small School
Favorable Draft Capital Deltas tend to lead to better outcomes in                               Studs

                                                                                                                Five school.

With previous versions of the guide, I’ve provided that raw Draft Capital
Delta number. You saw that Isiah Pacheco’s was high, and that Jonathan

Late-Round Fantasy Football: 2024 Prospect Guide
Jump To: ZAP Model | Year 2 Model                                                                                     24

                                               fi
                                 fl
                        ff
     ff
      Mingo’s wasn’t.

     What did those numbers even mean, though?

     That was a question I asked myself — the creator of these nerdy
     prospect formulas — this past year. Draft Capital Delta is neat and can
     be helpful, but what’s the di erence between a +10 and a -3? Does it
     matter that much?

     It seems pretty random, no?

     One of the issues with displaying Draft Capital Delta so prominently over
     the last couple of seasons was that some viewed it as being just as
     important as a player’s prospect score. That shouldn’t be the case. At
     all.

     A prospect with a score of 80 and a Draft Capital Delta of +7 is not better
     than a prospect with a score of 90 and a Draft Capital Delta of -3. It’s
     just that the player with the 90 score may have a little more risk to his
     pro le, since the model is viewing him less favorably than draft capital
     itself.

     Another key point to make is that Draft Capital Delta sees a larger range
     with later-round picks than it does with earlier-round ones. That’s due to
     draft capital getting more weight in the models for early-round
     selections.

     Think of it this way: If a running back is drafted 20th overall, no matter
     what his production pro le looks like, he’s going to have a high
     probability of scoring at least some fantasy points in the NFL. That’s not
     the case for a back who’s selected in Round 4 or Round 5. We have to
     lean more on the non-draft-capital aspects to his pro le, since teams are
     largely throwing darts on Day 3.

     So, with all that being noted, moving forward, we’re throwing out the
     individual, unique Draft Capital Delta numbers per player. That’s not
     what’s important. The ZAP score is.

Draft Capital Delta can help us nd gems, but it’s not the key metric we
should be working o of.

Adding Subjectivity
Dan Campbell chose to keep his o ense on the eld on fourth-and-three
in 49ers territory toward the end of this year’s NFC Championship game.
He could’ve attempted a eld goal, but, instead, he went for it.

“The analytics” said that the decision was essentially a coin ip. There
was no clear-cut, obvious answer.

“Greg from Twitter” said that Dan Campbell is a moron and that you
“have to take the points.”

The result of the play was an incomplete pass, but that conclusion isn’t
really what’s key. The process is. Campbell made a call based on the
information he had. And not all of that information was mathematical in
nature. Some of it was feel-based.

And that’s OK.

A fourth-down bot isn’t going to solve all of your problems. It’s merely
providing the probability of a particular outcome to go down. If, in the
moment, Campbell felt like his kicker wasn’t reliable, then he could —
and did — opt to go for it.

The data was there to let him know that either choice was reasonable.

You don’t have to agree with numbers all the time. I don’t agree with
numbers all the time.

 This is a guide, and the numbers — the prospect scores — are a guide,
too.

       Seeing how a player looks in a model can be
       helpful, but I’ll never tell you to stop there. Looking
       at a player’s journey and — gasp —
       watching the player play is always a solid idea as
       well.
You can follow the prospect models and nd success.

You can likely be even better with some rational thought piled on top.
